The files you extracted (`connect_device_package.zip`, `aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/`, the certs/keys) are for the **MQTT device connection** — that's a *different* thing from Secure Tunneling. The tunnel + those two access tokens use the **local proxy** (`localproxy`), not the Python SDK. Let me lay out how they fit together.

## The two roles

A secure tunnel has two ends:

| End | Runs where | Token | Mode |
|-----|-----------|-------|------|
| **destination** | on the **TurtleBot Pi** (the thing being reached) | `destinationAccessToken` | `localproxy -d` |
| **source** | on **your laptop** (where you initiate the connection) | `sourceAccessToken` | `localproxy -s` |

The destination forwards to a local service on the Pi (usually **SSH :22**); the source opens a local port on your laptop that tunnels to it. Then you `ssh ubuntu@localhost -p <port>`.

## You need the `localproxy` binary (not the Python SDK)

The Python SDK can't run a tunnel. You need **aws-iot-securetunneling-localproxy**: https://github.com/aws-samples/aws-iot-securetunneling-localproxy — build it (CMake) or grab a release binary, on **both** machines.

## Connect — destination side (on the Pi)

```bash
export AWSIOT_TUNNEL_ACCESS_TOKEN="<c9109663-destinationAccessToken value>"
localproxy -t $AWSIOT_TUNNEL_ACCESS_TOKEN \
  -r <region> \
  -d localhost:22          # forward inbound tunnel traffic to the Pi's SSH
```

`<region>` must match the region the tunnel was created in (e.g. `us-east-1`). Endpoint defaults to `data.tunneling.iot.<region>.amazonaws.com:443`.

## Connect — source side (on your laptop)

```bash
export AWSIOT_TUNNEL_ACCESS_TOKEN="<c9109663-sourceAccessToken value>"
localproxy -t $AWSIOT_TUNNEL_ACCESS_TOKEN \
  -r <region> \
  -s 5555                  # open local port 5555 on the laptop
```

Then from the laptop:

```bash
ssh ubuntu@localhost -p 5555     # lands on the Pi
```

## Two things to confirm

1. **Which region** did you create the tunnel in? (Tokens are region-scoped — wrong region = connection refused.)
2. **What service** do you want to reach over it — SSH (`:22`), or something else (e.g. a ROS bridge port)?

---

One alternative worth knowing: the message *"if a thing name was specified, the destination token will be delivered on the reserved MQTT topic"* means if the Pi runs the **IoT tunneling agent** subscribed to `$aws/things/<thing>/tunnels/notify`, the destination side connects **automatically** — you wouldn't paste the destination token at all, only run the source side. That agent uses the certs in your `connect_device_package` for the MQTT connection. That's the more "production" path; the manual `-d` command above is the simplest way to get connected right now.

Tell me the region and target service (and whether you're on the Pi or the laptop right now) and I'll give you the exact one-liner.

No Docker, cmake 3.22 present, ARM64 (Pi). So the path is **build `localproxy` from source**. Here's the full plan:

## On the Pi (destination) — build localproxy

```bash
sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y build-essential cmake \
  libboost-all-dev libssl-dev libprotobuf-dev protobuf-compiler \
  zlib1g-dev catch2

cd ~
git clone https://github.com/aws-samples/aws-iot-securetunneling-localproxy.git
cd aws-iot-securetunneling-localproxy
mkdir build && cd build
cmake ..            # if catch2 apt pkg is too old, cmake will tell you; can be worked around
make -j2            # Pi 4: a few minutes
# binary lands at: ~/aws-iot-securetunneling-localproxy/build/bin/localproxy
```

## Then connect — destination side (on the Pi)

```bash
cd ~/cs7980-guide-mate
export AWSIOT_TUNNEL_ACCESS_TOKEN="$(cat c9109663-destinationAccessToken.txt)"
~/aws-iot-securetunneling-localproxy/build/bin/localproxy \
  -t "$AWSIOT_TUNNEL_ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  -r us-west-2 \
  -d localhost:22
```

## Source side (on your laptop)

Copy `c9109663-sourceAccessToken.txt` to the laptop, build/install `localproxy` there too, then:

```bash
export AWSIOT_TUNNEL_ACCESS_TOKEN="$(cat c9109663-sourceAccessToken.txt)"

content.text:
Got it — let's step back from connecting and actually understand what AWS IoT Core *is*. I'll tie each feature to the files you already have, so it's concrete.

## What AWS IoT Core is

A managed cloud service that lets **devices** (your TurtleBots) and the **cloud** talk securely, mostly over **MQTT** (a lightweight publish/subscribe messaging protocol). Think of it as a secure message broker in the cloud plus a set of features built on top.

## Core building blocks

**1. Things & the Registry**
A "Thing" is a cloud representation of a device — e.g. `Turtlebot-468`. The registry stores metadata, groups, and attributes. It's just bookkeeping; it doesn't move data.

**2. Certificates + Policies (security)** ← *this is most of your directory*
- Every device authenticates with an **X.509 certificate** (your `Turtlebot-468.cert.pem` + `.private.key`) instead of a password.
- `root-CA.crt` verifies you're really talking to AWS (TLS).
- A **policy** (`Turtlebot-468-Policy`) says *what this cert is allowed to do* — which topics it can publish/subscribe to, whether it can connect. Yours currently allows only `sdk/test/*` topics. That's the deliberately-narrow quick-start policy.

**3. MQTT message broker (pub/sub)** ← *this is what `start.sh` exercises*
Devices **publish** messages to **topics** (like `turtlebot468/telemetry`) and **subscribe** to topics to receive them. The broker routes messages between publishers and subscribers — they never connect directly. Your `start.sh` runs the sample that publishes/subscribes on `sdk/test/python`. The endpoint `aqc6y1ij55nsq-ats.iot.us-west-2.amazonaws.com` is *your account's* broker address.

## Features built on top of the broker

**4. Device Shadows**
A JSON "shadow" document storing a device's *desired* vs *reported* state. You set "desired: lamp=on" in the cloud; the device syncs when it next connects. Useful for state you want even when the device is offline.

**5. Rules Engine**
SQL-like rules that react to messages: "when a message arrives on `turtlebot468/battery` and voltage < 12, write to DynamoDB / trigger a Lambda / send an SNS alert." This is how IoT data flows into the rest of AWS.

**6. Secure Tunneling** ← *the tunnel you set up earlier*
Lets you reach a device behind a firewall/NAT (your Pi on the lab network) **without opening any inbound ports** — e.g. SSH into the robot from home. Uses the source/destination tokens + `localproxy`, which is *separate* from the MQTT broker.

**7. Jobs**
Remote operations sent to fleets of devices — firmware updates, config changes, "run this command." Tracks rollout/success across many robots.

**8. Fleet Provisioning**
Automates onboarding many devices (generating certs at scale) — relevant if you go from 2 TurtleBots to 50.

**9. Greengrass** (you have a samples folder for it)
Runs IoT logic **on the device itself** (edge computing) — local processing when cloud connectivity is poor. Potentially interesting for a robot, since you don't want navigation depending on cloud latency.

## How your files map to all this

| Your file | IoT Core concept |
|-----------|------------------|
| `Turtlebot-468.cert.pem` / `.private.key` | Device identity (X.509 cert) |
| `root-CA.crt` | Verifies AWS's identity (TLS) |
| `Turtlebot-468-Policy` | What the device is allowed to do |
| `aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/` | Client library to talk MQTT |
| `start.sh` | Runs the pub/sub MQTT sample |
| `*AccessToken.txt` + `localproxy` | Secure Tunneling (remote access) |

## How it'd fit your TurtleBot project

Realistically useful pieces for a guide robot: **Secure Tunneling** (SSH in remotely without port-forwarding), **Jobs** (push code/config to both 468 and 436), and possibly **Rules + a dashboard** (stream battery/status telemetry to the cloud). The MQTT broker would *not* replace your ROS 2 / FastDDS networking on the robot — it's for **robot ↔ cloud**, not the robot's internal node-to-node traffic.

---

Want me to go deeper on any one of these — e.g. how MQTT topics/QoS actually work, how the cert+policy auth flow works step by step, or which features genuinely make sense for the guide-robot project?

content.text:
The main bridge is the **Rules Engine** — but there are a few distinct paths. Let me lay them out.

## 1. The Rules Engine (the primary path)

This is how ~90% of IoT-Core-to-AWS integration happens. A rule has three parts:

**a) A SQL statement** that selects messages from MQTT topics:
```sql
SELECT temperature, battery FROM 'turtlebot468/telemetry' WHERE battery < 12
```
It runs on every message hitting that topic, can filter (`WHERE`), and reshape the payload.

**b) One or more "Actions"** — where the matched message goes. Each action targets a specific AWS service:

| Action → Service | What it does |
|---|---|
| **Lambda** | Run arbitrary code (the most flexible — Lambda can then call *anything*) |
| **DynamoDB** | Write the message to a NoSQL table |
| **S3** | Store the payload as an object |
| **SNS** | Send a notification (SMS/email/push) |
| **SQS** | Queue the message for a consumer |
| **Kinesis / Firehose** | Stream into analytics/data lakes |
| **CloudWatch** | Push a metric or alarm |
| **Timestream** | Write to a time-series database |
| **republish** | Send it back to *another* MQTT topic |
| **Step Functions** | Kick off a workflow |

**c) An IAM role** — the rule assumes a role granting it permission to write to that target service. (This is separate from the *device* policy; it's the *rule's* permission.)

So the flow is:
```
Device → MQTT topic → Rule (SQL filter) → Action → AWS service
```

## 2. Lambda as the universal adapter

If no built-in action fits, you route to **Lambda**, and Lambda code calls any AWS API (or external HTTP). This is the escape hatch for custom logic — e.g. transform data, call a third-party API, write to a relational DB.

## 3. Service-to-device (the reverse direction)

Other services push *to* devices by **publishing to MQTT topics** via the IoT Data API (`iot-data:Publish`), or by updating a **Device Shadow**. So a Lambda can send a command to your robot by publishing to `turtlebot468/cmd`.

## 4. EventBridge (control-plane / lifecycle events)

IoT Core emits **lifecycle events** (device connected/disconnected, registry changes) to **EventBridge**, which can fan out to other services. Useful for "alert me when turtlebot468 goes offline."

## 5. IoT-native services that consume the data directly

- **IoT Analytics / IoT SiteWise** — managed pipelines for processing/storing telemetry.
- **IoT Events** — detect patterns across messages and trigger alerts.
- **Fleet Indexing** — makes the registry + shadows searchable.

## The mental model

IoT Core itself is mostly the **MQTT broker + security layer**. It doesn't store or analyze data on its own — the **Rules Engine is the router** that forwards messages outward, and **Lambda is the glue** when you need logic. Everything authenticates via **IAM roles** (for the rules/services) rather than the X.509 device certs (those are only for device↔broker).

```
                    ┌──→ Lambda ──→ (anything)
Device ──MQTT──→ IoT Core ──Rule──┼──→ DynamoDB / S3 / Timestream
                    │             ├──→ SNS / SQS / Kinesis
                    │             └──→ republish → another topic
                    └──lifecycle──→ EventBridge ──→ ...
```

---

For your TurtleBot case, a concrete example: robot publishes battery to `turtlebot468/battery` → a rule with `WHERE voltage < 12` → **SNS** action → you get a text when a robot needs docking. Want me to sketch out an actual rule + action for one of your project's use cases (battery alerts, status logging, or remote commands)?

message.content: can I access it from ec2 as well?

content.text:
Yes — and from EC2 you actually have an *extra*, easier option that physical devices don't: **IAM authentication**. Here's the breakdown.

## EC2 can talk to IoT Core three ways

**1. As an MQTT client using IAM (SigV4) — the easy way for AWS compute**
Because EC2 runs inside AWS, give the instance an **IAM role** (instance profile) and it can:
- **Publish** messages with a single API call — no certs needed:
  ```bash
  aws iot-data publish --topic 'turtlebot468/cmd' \
    --payload '{"action":"dock"}' --region us-west-2
  ```
- Connect over **MQTT-over-WebSocket** signed with the instance's IAM credentials.

The IAM role's permissions (e.g. `iot:Publish`, `iot:Connect`, `iot-data:*`) replace the device cert + IoT policy. This is the natural pattern for a backend app on EC2.

**2. As an MQTT client using X.509 certs — same as a device**
You *can* drop the same cert/key onto EC2 and connect exactly like the TurtleBot does (the SDK sample in your folder would run unchanged). But this is usually the wrong choice for EC2 — certs are for *devices*; IAM is for *AWS compute*.

**3. As a consumer of the data IoT Core forwards**
Often EC2 doesn't talk to IoT Core directly at all. The Rules Engine pushes messages into **SQS / DynamoDB / Kinesis**, and your EC2 app reads from those. This decouples the backend from the broker.

## Auth model — the key distinction

| Connecting from | Authenticates with |
|---|---|
| TurtleBot (device) | X.509 cert + IoT **policy** |
| EC2 / Lambda (AWS compute) | **IAM** role/credentials (SigV4) |

Both hit the same broker; they just prove identity differently.

## Two practical EC2 uses for your project

- **Secure Tunneling source end:** run `localproxy -s` on EC2 instead of your laptop, so you SSH to the robot through a stable cloud host. EC2 needs `localproxy` + the source token (or IAM perms to *open* tunnels itself via `iot:OpenTunnel`).
- **Backend / dashboard:** EC2 publishes commands to the robots and ingests their telemetry (directly via MQTT, or via SQS fed by a rule).

---

So: EC2 is a first-class IoT Core client, and with an IAM role it's simpler than the device path (no cert files to manage). Want me to show the IAM-role + WebSocket subscribe setup, or the `aws iot-data` publish/subscribe quick path?

2	
3	This section documents the network setup for the cs7980 TurtleBot 4 robots.
4	
5	## Documents
6	- [Connecting a TurtleBot 4 to NUwave](nuwave-connection.md)
7	- [ROS 2 over NUwave — what works and what doesn't](ros2-over-nuwave.md)
8	
9	## The two-computer architecture
10	A TurtleBot 4 is **two networked computers**:
11	
12	| Component | Role | WiFi capability |
13	|---|---|---|
14	| **Raspberry Pi 4** | Runs Ubuntu 22.04 + ROS 2 Humble. The host you SSH into. | 2.4 + 5 GHz (dual-band) |
15	| **iRobot Create 3 base** | Mobility base (wheels, IMU, bumpers, docking). | 2.4 GHz only, **WPA2-Personal only** |
16	
17	They are joined by the **USB-C cable**, which carries both power and a **wired Ethernet link** on the `192.168.186.0/24` subnet (Pi = `.3`, Create 3 = `.2`).
18	
19	### Why the Create 3 never joins NUwave
20	The Create 3's firmware does **not** support WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X / PEAP) — it can only join a network with a single shared password. NUwave is enterprise, so the Create 3 **cannot** join it. This is fine because the robots use **Discovery Server** mode: the Create 3 keeps WiFi disabled and routes everything through the Pi over the USB-C link. Only the **Pi** connects to NUwave.
21	
22	## Discovery model: FastDDS Discovery Server
23	ROS 2's default discovery is **multicast**, which is link-local and **will not cross IP subnets**. On NUwave the Pi and a laptop typically land on **different `/19` segments**, so multicast discovery is a non-starter.
24	
25	The robots instead run a **FastDDS Discovery Server** on the Pi (`fast-discovery-server -i 0 -p 11811`, listening on `0.0.0.0:11811`). This uses **unicast**, which routes across subnets. Clients (the Pi's own ROS nodes, and any laptop) point at the server via `ROS_DISCOVERY_SERVER`.
26	
27	Relevant environment on the Pi (`/etc/turtlebot4/setup.bash`):
28	```
29	ROS_DOMAIN_ID=0
30	RMW_IMPLEMENTATION=rmw_fastrtps_cpp
31	ROS_LOCALHOST_ONLY=0
32	ROS_DISCOVERY_SERVER=127.0.0.1:11811;
33	FASTRTPS_DEFAULT_PROFILES_FILE=/etc/turtlebot4/fastdds_rpi.xml
34	```
35	> **Cleaned up 2026-06-12:** stale `192.168.50.x` entries from a previous network were
36	> removed from three places on robot 468: the Pi's `ROS_DISCOVERY_SERVER`
37	> (`192.168.50.31:11811`), the **Create 3's own discovery-server address** (same stale
38	> IP — this one was *not* harmless: it silenced every base topic; see
39	> [ros2-over-nuwave.md](ros2-over-nuwave.md)), and chrony's preferred NTP server
40	> (`192.168.50.146` → `pool.ntp.org`, verified working over NUwave). After any future
41	> network change, check for relics: `grep -r "192.168.50" /etc/`
42	> Robot **436 has not been migrated or cleaned up yet** — same steps apply.
43	
44	## Time sync
45	Chain: internet NTP (`pool.ntp.org`, with `ntp.ubuntu.com` backup) → Pi (chrony) →
46	Create 3 (chrony serves `192.168.186.0/24`). **Gotcha:** the Pi has **no RTC** — it
47	boots with a stale clock (whatever it was at last shutdown) until chrony steps it once
48	WiFi connects. Services started before the step see a huge time jump; check
49	`chronyc tracking` when debugging anything time-related.
50	
51	## Addressing summary (as configured June 2026)
52	| Host | NUwave IP (example) | Notes |
53	|---|---|---|
54	| `turtlebot468` Pi | `10.247.204.21/19` | DHCP — will change |
55	| Create 3 (468) | `192.168.186.2` | wired-only, via Pi |
56	| Lab laptop | `10.247.228.155/19` | different `/19` than the Pi |
57	
58	> NUwave IPs are DHCP and **change** on reconnect. The robot shows its current IP on its display. On the lab `ASUS_98` network the Pis were previously `10.91.221.14` (468) and `10.91.221.95` (436).
59

# Network Overview

This section documents the network setup for the cs7980 TurtleBot 4 robots.

## Documents
- [Connecting a TurtleBot 4 to NUwave](nuwave-connection.md)
- [ROS 2 over NUwave — what works and what doesn't](ros2-over-nuwave.md)

## The two-computer architecture
A TurtleBot 4 is **two networked computers**:

| Component | Role | WiFi capability |
|---|---|---|
| **Raspberry Pi 4** | Runs Ubuntu 22.04 + ROS 2 Humble. The host you SSH into. | 2.4 + 5 GHz (dual-band) |
| **iRobot Create 3 base** | Mobility base (wheels, IMU, bumpers, docking). | 2.4 GHz only, **WPA2-Personal only** |

They are joined by the **USB-C cable**, which carries both power and a **wired Ethernet link** on the `192.168.186.0/24` subnet (Pi = `.3`, Create 3 = `.2`).

### Why the Create 3 never joins NUwave
The Create 3's firmware does **not** support WPA2-Enterprise (802.1X / PEAP) — it can only join a network with a single shared password. NUwave is enterprise, so the Create 3 **cannot** join it. This is fine because the robots use **Discovery Server** mode: the Create 3 keeps WiFi disabled and routes everything through the Pi over the USB-C link. Only the **Pi** connects to NUwave.

## Discovery model: FastDDS Discovery Server
ROS 2's default discovery is **multicast**, which is link-local and **will not cross IP subnets**. On NUwave the Pi and a laptop typically land on **different `/19` segments**, so multicast discovery is a non-starter.

The robots instead run a **FastDDS Discovery Server** on the Pi (`fast-discovery-server -i 0 -p 11811`, listening on `0.0.0.0:11811`). This uses **unicast**, which routes across subnets. Clients (the Pi's own ROS nodes, and any laptop) point at the server via `ROS_DISCOVERY_SERVER`.

Relevant environment on the Pi (`/etc/turtlebot4/setup.bash`):
```
ROS_DOMAIN_ID=0
RMW_IMPLEMENTATION=rmw_fastrtps_cpp
ROS_LOCALHOST_ONLY=0
ROS_DISCOVERY_SERVER=127.0.0.1:11811;
FASTRTPS_DEFAULT_PROFILES_FILE=/etc/turtlebot4/fastdds_rpi.xml
```
> **Cleaned up 2026-06-12:** stale `192.168.50.x` entries from a previous network were
> removed from three places on robot 468: the Pi's `ROS_DISCOVERY_SERVER`
> (`192.168.50.31:11811`), the **Create 3's own discovery-server address** (same stale
> IP — this one was *not* harmless: it silenced every base topic; see
> [ros2-over-nuwave.md](ros2-over-nuwave.md)), and chrony's preferred NTP server
> (`192.168.50.146` → `pool.ntp.org`, verified working over NUwave). After any future
> network change, check for relics: `grep -r "192.168.50" /etc/`
> Robot **436 has not been migrated or cleaned up yet** — same steps apply.

## Time sync
Chain: internet NTP (`pool.ntp.org`, with `ntp.ubuntu.com` backup) → Pi (chrony) →
Create 3 (chrony serves `192.168.186.0/24`). **Gotcha:** the Pi has **no RTC** — it
boots with a stale clock (whatever it was at last shutdown) until chrony steps it once
WiFi connects. Services started before the step see a huge time jump; check
`chronyc tracking` when debugging anything time-related.

## Addressing summary (as configured June 2026)
| Host | NUwave IP (example) | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `turtlebot468` Pi | `10.247.204.21/19` | DHCP — will change |
| Create 3 (468) | `192.168.186.2` | wired-only, via Pi |
| Lab laptop | `10.247.228.155/19` | different `/19` than the Pi |

> NUwave IPs are DHCP and **change** on reconnect. The robot shows its current IP on its display. On the lab `ASUS_98` network the Pis were previously `10.91.221.14` (468) and `10.91.221.95` (436).

# AWS IoT Core — Overview

How AWS IoT Core fits the cs7980 guide-robot project, and what we set up on robot **468**.

> ⚠️ **Security:** none of the device credentials live in this repo. The certificate,
> private key, root CA, IoT policy, tunnel access tokens, and the AWS "connect a device"
> package are **gitignored** (see the repo `.gitignore`). Docs below use placeholders such
> as `<account-id>`, `<your-ats-endpoint>`, and `<region>`. The real values for our setup
> are in Claude memory (`aws-iot-setup`), not here.

## TL;DR
- **IoT Core = a managed MQTT message broker + a device security layer**, plus features
  built on top (shadows, rules, jobs, secure tunneling).
- It does **not** store or analyze data itself — the **Rules Engine** routes messages out
  to other AWS services, and **Lambda** is the glue for custom logic.
- For the robots it is **robot ↔ cloud** plumbing. It does **not** replace the on-robot
  ROS 2 / FastDDS networking (that stays node-to-node; see [network docs](../network/README.md)).
- We have the quick-connect MQTT sample working and a **Secure Tunnel** opened, but
  `localproxy` is **not yet installed**, so the tunnel is not usable end-to-end yet.

## Documents
- [What IoT Core is — building blocks](iot-core-overview.md) — things, certs/policies, the MQTT broker, shadows, rules, jobs, Greengrass; and how our files map to each.
- [Talking to other AWS services + EC2 access](service-integration.md) — the Rules Engine, service-to-device, EventBridge, and the three ways EC2 can reach IoT Core.
- [Secure Tunneling — remote SSH to a robot](secure-tunneling.md) — source/destination tokens, `localproxy`, the MQTT auto-delivery path, and why it isn't connected yet.

## What we set up (robot 468)
| Piece | State |
|---|---|
| Thing in the registry | `Turtlebot-468` (region `<region>`) |
| Device cert + private key | downloaded to the Pi (gitignored) |
| IoT policy | quick-start scope — **only `sdk/test/*` topics**; no tunnel/shadow topics yet |
| MQTT pub/sub sample | runs via `start.sh` (publishes to `sdk/test/python`) |
| Secure Tunnel | opened; source + destination tokens downloaded (gitignored) |
| `localproxy` | **not installed** — required to actually use the tunnel |

## Where IoT Core could help the project
| Use case | Feature |
|---|---|
| SSH into a robot behind the lab firewall, no port-forwarding | **Secure Tunneling** |
| Push code/config to both 468 and 436 | **Jobs** |
| Stream battery/status telemetry to a dashboard or alert | **Rules Engine → SNS / DynamoDB / Timestream** |
| Send a command to a robot from a backend | **publish to an MQTT topic** (Lambda/EC2 via IAM) |

See the per-topic docs for detail.

# What AWS IoT Core Is — Building Blocks

A managed cloud service that lets **devices** (our TurtleBots) and the **cloud** exchange
messages securely, mostly over **MQTT** (a lightweight publish/subscribe protocol). It is a
secure message broker plus a set of features layered on top.

## Core building blocks

### 1. Things & the Registry
A "Thing" is the cloud's record of a device — e.g. `Turtlebot-468`. The registry stores
metadata, attributes, and group membership. It is bookkeeping only; it does not move data.

### 2. Certificates + Policies (security)
- Each device authenticates with an **X.509 certificate** (cert + private key) instead of a
  password.
- The **root CA** lets the device verify it is really talking to AWS (TLS).
- An **IoT policy** says *what that certificate may do* — which topics it can publish to /
  subscribe to, whether it may connect. Our policy is the narrow quick-start scope: it
  allows only `sdk/test/{python,java,js}` topics and a few client IDs. Anything else (tunnel
  notify topics, shadows, custom telemetry topics) would require widening the policy.

### 3. MQTT message broker (publish / subscribe)
Devices **publish** messages to **topics** (e.g. `turtlebot468/telemetry`) and **subscribe**
to topics to receive them. The broker routes between publishers and subscribers — they never
connect directly. Each AWS account has its own broker endpoint (the **ATS endpoint**,
`<your-ats-endpoint>` of the form `xxxx-ats.iot.<region>.amazonaws.com`).

**QoS** (quality of service) levels: `0` = at most once (fire-and-forget), `1` = at least
once (may duplicate). IoT Core does not support QoS 2.

## Features built on top of the broker

### 4. Device Shadows
A JSON document holding a device's **desired** vs **reported** state. The cloud sets
"desired", the device reconciles when it next connects. Good for state you want to set even
while the device is offline.

### 5. Rules Engine
SQL-like rules that react to messages and forward them to other AWS services. This is the
main integration path — see [service-integration.md](service-integration.md).

### 6. Secure Tunneling
Reach a device behind a firewall/NAT **without opening inbound ports** (e.g. SSH into a robot
on the lab network). Separate from the MQTT broker. See
[secure-tunneling.md](secure-tunneling.md).

### 7. Jobs
Remote operations dispatched to fleets — firmware updates, config changes, "run this
command" — with rollout/success tracking across many devices.

### 8. Fleet Provisioning
Automates onboarding many devices at scale (cert generation, registration). Relevant if the
fleet grows beyond the two lab robots.

### 9. Greengrass
Runs IoT logic **on the device itself** (edge compute) for local processing when cloud
connectivity is poor. Potentially relevant for a robot, where navigation must not depend on
cloud latency.

## How our files map to these concepts
| File on the Pi (gitignored) | IoT Core concept |
|---|---|
| `Turtlebot-468.cert.pem` / `.private.key` | Device identity (X.509 cert) |
| `root-CA.crt` | Verifies AWS's identity (TLS) |
| `Turtlebot-468-Policy` | What the device is allowed to do |
| `aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/` | Client library to speak MQTT |
| `start.sh` | Runs the pub/sub MQTT sample (`sdk/test/python`) |
| `*AccessToken.txt` | Secure Tunneling source/destination tokens |

## Mental model
IoT Core itself is mostly the **MQTT broker + security layer**. It does not store or analyze
data on its own — the **Rules Engine routes** messages outward and **Lambda is the glue**.
Device↔broker auth uses **X.509 certs + IoT policy**; AWS-internal compute (Lambda, EC2)
uses **IAM** instead.

# IoT Core ↔ Other AWS Services (and EC2 Access)

How messages get from the MQTT broker into the rest of AWS, how services push back to
devices, and how an EC2 instance can talk to IoT Core.

## The Rules Engine (the primary path)
~90% of IoT-Core-to-AWS integration goes through rules. A rule has three parts:

**a) A SQL statement** selecting messages from topics:
```sql
SELECT temperature, battery FROM 'turtlebot468/telemetry' WHERE battery < 12
```
It runs on every matching message, can filter (`WHERE`) and reshape the payload.

**b) One or more Actions** — where the matched message goes:

| Action → Service | What it does |
|---|---|
| **Lambda** | Run arbitrary code (most flexible — can then call anything) |
| **DynamoDB** | Write the message to a NoSQL table |
| **S3** | Store the payload as an object |
| **SNS** | Send a notification (SMS / email / push) |
| **SQS** | Queue the message for a consumer |
| **Kinesis / Firehose** | Stream into analytics / a data lake |
| **CloudWatch** | Push a metric or alarm |
| **Timestream** | Write to a time-series database |
| **republish** | Send it to another MQTT topic |
| **Step Functions** | Start a workflow |

**c) An IAM role** the rule assumes to write to the target. This is the *rule's* permission,
separate from the *device's* IoT policy.

```
Device → MQTT topic → Rule (SQL filter) → Action → AWS service
```

## Lambda as the universal adapter
If no built-in action fits, route to **Lambda**; its code can call any AWS API or external
HTTP endpoint. The escape hatch for custom logic (transform, call a third-party API, write to
a relational DB).

## Service → device (the reverse direction)
Other services push *to* devices by **publishing to MQTT topics** via the IoT Data API
(`iot-data:Publish`) or by updating a **Device Shadow**. So a Lambda can command a robot by
publishing to e.g. `turtlebot468/cmd`.

## EventBridge (lifecycle / control-plane events)
IoT Core emits lifecycle events (device connected/disconnected, registry changes) to
**EventBridge**, which fans out to other services — e.g. "alert when turtlebot468 goes
offline."

## IoT-native consumers
- **IoT Analytics / IoT SiteWise** — managed telemetry pipelines/storage.
- **IoT Events** — detect patterns across messages and trigger.
- **Fleet Indexing** — makes the registry + shadows searchable.

```
                    ┌──→ Lambda ──→ (anything)
Device ──MQTT──→ IoT Core ──Rule──┼──→ DynamoDB / S3 / Timestream
                    │             ├──→ SNS / SQS / Kinesis
                    │             └──→ republish → another topic
                    └──lifecycle──→ EventBridge ──→ ...
```

---

## Accessing IoT Core from EC2
EC2 is a first-class IoT Core client, and because it runs **inside AWS** it has an easier
option than physical devices: **IAM authentication** (no cert files to manage).

### 1. As an MQTT client using IAM (SigV4) — the easy way for AWS compute
Give the instance an **IAM role** (instance profile), then:
- **Publish** with one call, no certs:
  ```bash
  aws iot-data publish --topic 'turtlebot468/cmd' \
    --payload '{"action":"dock"}' --region <region>
  ```
- Or connect over **MQTT-over-WebSocket** signed with the instance's IAM credentials.

The IAM role's permissions (`iot:Connect`, `iot:Publish`, `iot-data:*`, …) replace the
device cert + IoT policy.

### 2. As an MQTT client using X.509 certs — same as a device
You *can* copy the same cert/key onto EC2 and connect exactly like the robot does (the SDK
sample would run unchanged). Usually the wrong choice for EC2 — certs are for devices, IAM is
for AWS compute.

### 3. As a consumer of forwarded data
Often EC2 does not touch IoT Core directly: a rule pushes messages into **SQS / DynamoDB /
Kinesis**, and the EC2 app reads from there. This decouples the backend from the broker.

### Auth model — the key distinction
| Connecting from | Authenticates with |
|---|---|
| TurtleBot (device) | X.509 cert + IoT **policy** |
| EC2 / Lambda (AWS compute) | **IAM** role / credentials (SigV4) |

Both reach the same broker; they just prove identity differently.

### Two practical EC2 uses for this project
- **Secure Tunneling source end:** run `localproxy -s` on EC2 instead of a laptop, for a
  stable cloud host to SSH through to the robot (EC2 needs the source token, or
  `iot:OpenTunnel` to open tunnels itself). See [secure-tunneling.md](secure-tunneling.md).
- **Backend / dashboard:** EC2 publishes commands to the robots and ingests telemetry
  (directly via MQTT, or via SQS fed by a rule).

input.content:
# AWS IoT Secure Tunneling — Remote SSH to a Robot

Reach a robot that sits behind the lab firewall/NAT **without opening any inbound port** —
e.g. SSH into the Pi from home. This is separate from the MQTT broker; it uses a small
program called **`localproxy`** plus a pair of access tokens.

## TL;DR
- A tunnel has **two ends**: a **destination** (the robot) and a **source** (your laptop or
  an EC2 host). Each gets its own single-use **access token**.
- You need the **`localproxy`** binary on **both** ends. The Python device SDK / the
  "connect a device" package **cannot** do tunneling.
- **Status for us:** tunnel opened and both tokens downloaded, but `localproxy` is **not
  installed**, so the tunnel is not yet usable.

## The two ends
| End | Runs where | Token | `localproxy` mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **destination** | on the **robot Pi** (the thing being reached) | destination token | `-d localhost:22` |
| **source** | on your **laptop / EC2** (where you initiate) | source token | `-s <local-port>` |

The destination forwards inbound tunnel traffic to a local service on the robot (usually
**SSH :22**). The source opens a local port that tunnels to it; you then SSH to that local
port and land on the robot.

## Install `localproxy`
Not bundled with the device SDK. Build from source
(<https://github.com/aws-samples/aws-iot-securetunneling-localproxy>) or use the Docker image,
on **both** ends. On the Pi (ARM64, Ubuntu 22.04) build from source:

```bash
sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y build-essential cmake \
  libboost-all-dev libssl-dev libprotobuf-dev protobuf-compiler zlib1g-dev catch2
cd ~ && git clone https://github.com/aws-samples/aws-iot-securetunneling-localproxy.git
cd aws-iot-securetunneling-localproxy && mkdir build && cd build
cmake .. && make -j2
# binary: ~/aws-iot-securetunneling-localproxy/build/bin/localproxy
```

## Connect — destination side (on the robot Pi)
```bash
export AWSIOT_TUNNEL_ACCESS_TOKEN="$(cat <destination-token-file>)"
localproxy -t "$AWSIOT_TUNNEL_ACCESS_TOKEN" -r <region> -d localhost:22
```

## Connect — source side (on your laptop / EC2)
```bash
export AWSIOT_TUNNEL_ACCESS_TOKEN="$(cat <source-token-file>)"
localproxy -t "$AWSIOT_TUNNEL_ACCESS_TOKEN" -r <region> -s 5555
ssh ubuntu@localhost -p 5555     # lands on the robot
```

`<region>` **must** match the region the tunnel was created in, or the connection is refused.
The tunnel data endpoint defaults to `data.tunneling.iot.<region>.amazonaws.com:443`.

## The MQTT auto-delivery path (optional, "production" style)
When a tunnel is opened with a **destination thing name**, AWS publishes the destination
token to the reserved MQTT topic `$aws/things/<thing>/tunnels/notify`. If the robot runs an
agent subscribed to that topic (e.g. the **AWS IoT Device Client**, or a small listener using
the device SDK), the destination side connects **automatically** — you never paste the
destination token, only run the source side.

**Two prerequisites we do not yet meet:**
1. The robot's **IoT policy** must allow `iot:Subscribe` / `iot:Receive` on
   `$aws/things/<thing>/tunnels/notify` (our quick-start policy only allows `sdk/test/*`).
2. `localproxy` must still be installed — the agent only fetches the token and *launches* it.

Since we already downloaded the destination token to the Pi, the manual `-d` command above is
the simplest way to connect; the MQTT path mainly matters for unattended fleets.

## Gotchas
- **Tokens are single-use.** A failed connect attempt burns them — **rotate/resend** tokens
  from the console to get a fresh pair before retrying.
- **`localproxy` is the missing piece**, not the SDK. Tokens alone do nothing.
- **No inbound ports needed** — both ends dial *out* to AWS over 443; that is the whole point
  versus port-forwarding.

## Status (robot 468)
- ✅ Tunnel opened; source + destination tokens downloaded (gitignored).
- ✅ Region and thing identified (see Claude memory `aws-iot-setup`).
- ❌ `localproxy` not installed on the Pi or laptop → tunnel not usable end-to-end yet.
- ❌ IoT policy does not yet permit the tunnel notify topic (only needed for the auto-delivery
  path).

# cs7980 Guide-Mate — Documentation

Working documentation for the cs7980 TurtleBot 4 project.

## Index

### Network
- [Network overview](network/README.md) — how the robots are connected, addressing, discovery model
- [Connecting a TurtleBot 4 to NUwave](network/nuwave-connection.md) — step-by-step, including the 5 GHz fix that was the real blocker
- [ROS 2 over NUwave — what works and what doesn't](network/ros2-over-nuwave.md) — compatibility findings

### Camera
- [Camera overview](camera/README.md) — the OAK-D-LITE depth/RGB camera
- [OAK-D-LITE camera — test, findings, fixes, pending issues](camera/oak-d-camera-test.md) — both streams work on USB 2; USB 3 boot-loop root-caused to power; bandwidth-limited frame drops

### Mapping & navigation
- [Autonomous mapping — overview](mapping/README.md) — BFS frontier explorer + SLAM + Nav2, glass handling, how to run, status
- [Depth camera for mapping](mapping/depth-perception.md) — using the OAK-D depth to see glass the lidar can't: FOV, the height-filtered pipeline, compute, and the planned lidar-scan injection
- [Viewing live mapping in RViz](mapping/rviz-visualization.md) — laptop-side RViz for the map, lidar, depth scan and cloud; the tf-remap, QoS, and Lite-vs-Standard model gotchas
- Code: [`src/guide_mate_explorer`](../src/guide_mate_explorer) — the `bfs_explorer` and `glass_guard` nodes

### Power & battery
- [Power overview](power/README.md) — battery-powered robot; only `battery_state` metering; ~14 W idle
- [Power consumption & saving](power/power-saving.md) — measured draw, what's always running, and the working "park" (stop SLAM → lidar auto-idles + CPU freed); what does *not* work

---

## ⚠️ Security note
**Do not commit credentials to this repo.** NUwave usernames/passwords, robot passwords, and any private keys must stay out of version control. The docs use placeholders such as `<nuwave-username>` and `<nuwave-password>`. Keep real values in a local, untracked file (e.g. `secrets.local`, already gitignored) or a password manager.

## Quick facts
- **Robots:** two TurtleBot 4 units — `turtlebot468` (room 468) and `turtlebot436` (room 436).
- **Each robot = two computers:** a Raspberry Pi 4 (Ubuntu 22.04, ROS 2 Humble — the thing you SSH into) and an iRobot Create 3 base (joined to the Pi by USB-C, which is both power and a wired Ethernet link).
- **Discovery model:** FastDDS **Discovery Server** running on the Pi; the Create 3 stays off WiFi and talks to the Pi over the USB-C link.
- **Default fallback network:** a local ASUS router, SSID `ASUS_98` (the robots auto-connect to it).

---
name: aws-iot-setup
description: AWS IoT Core setup for robot 468 — thing, region, endpoint, policy scope, secure tunnel state
metadata:
  type: project
---

AWS IoT Core was set up for robot **468** (the cloud-connectivity track of the guide-mate project). Full write-up lives in the repo at `docs/aws-iot/`. Project-specific values (kept here, not in the repo, since the repo bans credentials):

- **Thing name:** `Turtlebot-468`
- **Region:** `us-west-2`
- **Account ID:** `852373397000`
- **MQTT (ATS) endpoint:** `aqc6y1ij55nsq-ats.iot.us-west-2.amazonaws.com`
- **Device files on the Pi** (gitignored, in repo root): `Turtlebot-468.cert.pem`, `Turtlebot-468.private.key`, `Turtlebot-468.public.key`, `root-CA.crt`, `Turtlebot-468-Policy`, `connect_device_package.zip`, `aws-iot-device-sdk-python-v2/`, `start.sh`.
- **IoT policy scope:** quick-start only — allows `iot:Publish/Receive/Subscribe` on `sdk/test/{python,java,js}` and connect for a few sample client IDs. Does **not** allow tunnel notify, shadow, or custom telemetry topics yet.
- **MQTT sample:** `start.sh` runs the pub/sub sample on topic `sdk/test/python`.

**Secure Tunneling state (as of 2026-06-19):**
- A tunnel was opened; both access tokens downloaded to repo root as `c9109663-sourceAccessToken.txt` / `c9109663-destinationAccessToken.txt` (gitignored, single-use).
- **`localproxy` is NOT installed** on the Pi or laptop — this is the blocker; tokens alone do nothing. The Python device SDK cannot do tunneling.
- The Pi is ARM64 (aarch64); no Docker installed; cmake 3.22 present → build localproxy from source.
- MQTT auto-delivery of the destination token (reserved topic `$aws/things/Turtlebot-468/tunnels/notify`) would also need the IoT policy widened.

**Why:** these are account/region/identity facts not derivable from the (credential-free) repo, needed to resume the tunnel/MQTT work.
**How to apply:** to finish the tunnel, install localproxy both ends, run `-d localhost:22` on the Pi and `-s <port>` on the source, region `us-west-2`. See `docs/aws-iot/secure-tunneling.md`. Related: [[guide-mate-explorer]].

content.text:
Done. Everything is saved, documented, and pushed.

## What I did

**Docs (committed & pushed)** — new `docs/aws-iot/` matching the house style:
- `README.md` — overview + what we set up on 468 + security note
- `iot-core-overview.md` — building blocks (things, certs/policies, MQTT broker, shadows, rules, jobs, Greengrass) + how your files map to each
- `service-integration.md` — Rules Engine, service→device, EventBridge, **and the EC2 access section** (IAM vs cert auth)
- `secure-tunneling.md` — localproxy, source/dest tokens, MQTT auto-delivery, and why it's not connected yet
- Updated `docs/README.md` index

**Memory (local, not pushed)** — `aws-iot-setup.md` holds the real specifics (thing `Turtlebot-468`, region `us-west-2`, endpoint, account ID, policy scope, tunnel state) + a MEMORY.md index line.

**Security** — hardened `.gitignore` to permanently block `*.pem`, `*.key`, `*.crt`, `*-Policy`, `*AccessToken*`, the device package, the SDK clone, and `start.sh`. I verified the staged diff contained **no** private keys, tokens, endpoints, or account IDs — docs use placeholders only.

**Pushed** to branch `docs/aws-iot-core`. I branched rather than committing straight to `main` (default-branch safety). Open the PR here, or I can merge to `main` for you:
https://github.com/KalharPandya/cs7980-guide-mate/pull/new/docs/aws-iot-core

The unrelated in-progress work (`combined_node.py`, `combined.launch.py`, `setup.py`, `src/guide_mate_perception/`) was left untouched.

## ⚠️ One thing you should act on
Your git remote URL has a **GitHub personal access token embedded in it** (`ghp_…`), which showed up in `git remote -v`. That token is exposed to anyone who can see your shell/config. I'd recommend **rotating that PAT** on GitHub and switching to a credential helper or SSH remote so it isn't stored in plaintext in `.git/config`. Want me to reconfigure the remote to use SSH (or the git credential helper) so the token isn't sitting in the URL?
